rules: Use subject pronouns before verbs; use object pronouns after verbs or prepositions.
Use 'have got' to talk about possession.
Use 'can' to show ability or to ask for permission.

vocabulary.context: Hi, I am Pat. And I am a pretty little cat.
This is Chuck. And he is a funny little duck.

vocabulary.meaning: Những con chuột (số nhiều của mouse)
vocabulary.context: They are Bob and Alice. And they are little mice.
Is he a clown? Yes, he is.
I am thirsty.
He has got fair hair and glasses.

rules: Nouns ending in -s, -ss, -sh, -ch, -x, -o add -es.
Nouns ending in consonant + -y change to -ies.
Demonstratives: This/These for things near; That/Those for things far.
Use 'a/an' only with singular countable nouns. Use 'some' with plurals and uncountable nouns.

vocabulary.context: We form the plural of most nouns: cameras.
Nouns ending in -f/-fe -> -ves: thief -> thieves.
Irregular plurals: child -> children.
Some nouns are uncountable. They have no plural (e.g. bread, cheese, water).
A loaf of bread / two loaves of bread.
A carton of milk / two cartons of milk.

patterns: the + Noun + of + Noun (e.g., the trunk of the tree)
rules: Possessive adjectives must be followed by nouns; possessive pronouns are used alone.
Use 's for one person/animal; use s' for two or more people.
Use 'of' to show possession for things, not 's.

vocabulary.context: Possessive Adjectives vs Possessive Pronouns.
The lion's mane.
Whose hat is it? It's Mike's.
The trunk of the tree.
Where is the roof of my car?

units.title: There is / There are - Some / Any

patterns: Is there any + Singular Uncountable Noun?
Are there any + Plural Countable Noun?
There isn't any / There aren't any
rules: Use 'some' in affirmative sentences for plurals and uncountables.
Use 'any' in questions and negative sentences.

vocabulary.context: In Choco Town, there’s a baker’s and a bank.
There are two sweet shops but there aren’t any trees.
Is there a theatre in Hallam Street?
Look at the picture and write sentences: There is some flour.
Are there five lollipops?

rules: Use Present Continuous for actions happening right now at the moment of speaking.
Spelling: verb ending in consonant + vowel + consonant doubles the last letter (run -> running).
Spelling: drop silent -e (dance -> dancing), change -ie to -y (lie -> lying).

vocabulary.context: The children are fighting.
Mime an action. Your friends try to guess.
Father is digging in the garden.
It's winter but it isn't snowing today.
The sun is shining today.

patterns: Subject + verb 'to be' + Adverb of Frequency
rules: Use Present Simple for habits, routines, repeated actions, and permanent states.
Spelling for he/she/it: verbs ending in -ss, -sh, -ch, -x, -o add -es (wash -> washes).
Adverbs of frequency go before main verbs but after the verb 'to be'.

vocabulary.context: Mary, Mary, quite contrary. How does your garden grow?
We use Present Simple for permanent actions and routines.
I seldom help in the house.
Mother always washes the dishes.
My father often tells good jokes.

rules: Use imperatives to tell someone to do or not to do something.
Imperatives do not have a subject (the subject is understood as 'you').

vocabulary.context: We use the imperative when we tell someone to do something.
Don't shout!
I can't reach the biscuits!
Take your boots off, please!
Stop fighting!

patterns: in / on / under / next to / behind / in front of / between / opposite / above / below
rules: Use prepositions of place to describe where people, animals, or things are located.

vocabulary.context: Opposite the box / there was a park opposite our house.
Between two boxes / Peter is sitting between his mother and father.
Above the box / There is a poster above the bed.
Below the box / The cat is below the shelf.
Can you see him? He's on a rug next to the bed.

rules: Use 'in' for long periods and parts of the day.
Use 'at' for precise times and special expressions.
Use 'on' for specific days and dates.
Use 'before' and 'after' to specify the order of events.

vocabulary.context: Independence Day is on July 4th.
I get dressed before I have breakfast.
On / In autumn.
At midnight.
At a quarter past six.

patterns: How many + Plural Countable Noun + are there?
How much + Uncountable Noun + is there?
A lot! / Not many! / Not much!
rules: Use 'How many' for countable nouns in plural.
Use 'How much' for uncountable nouns.
Answer with 'Not many' for countable and 'Not much' for uncountable.

vocabulary.context: Countable or Uncountable: pasta.
Countable or Uncountable: yoghurt.
How much wool do you need to make a sweater? A lot!
None. Sheep can't knit!
I want to make an apple pie. Can you help me?

patterns: Subject + am/is/are + not + going to + Verb
rules: Use 'be going to' to talk about plans, intentions, and decisions made before speaking.
Use 'be going to' when there is clear evidence in the present that something will happen soon.

vocabulary.context: We use be going to to talk about plans and intentions.

vocabulary.meaning: Bằng chứng, dấu hiệu rõ ràng
vocabulary.context: Use be going to when there is evidence that something is going to happen.
Are you going to visit the Statue of Liberty?
Are you going to get some autographs?
Look at Julie's diary and write what her plans are.

units.title: Like / Love / Hate + -ing form - Want + to form

rules: Use the -ing form (gerund) after verbs of emotion like like, love, hate, enjoy.
Use the to-infinitive form after the verb want.

vocabulary.context: Dad, do you like eating baked apples?
I want to report a burglary. I'm inside a bathroom.
The burglar!
Sue and Beth want to be dentists.
Adam wants to be a pilot.

units.title: Must - Mustn’t - Have to - Shall - May

rules: Use 'must' for obligation/necessity.
Use 'mustn't' for prohibition (not allowed).
Use 'have to' when it is necessary/obligatory (rules, external laws).
Use 'don't have to' when something is not necessary (optional).
Use 'shall I' to offer help; use 'may I' to ask for permission politely.

vocabulary.context: We use must to talk about obligation or necessity.
We use mustn't to talk about prohibition.
You mustn't swim in the pond.
No Rubbish. Don't throw rubbish on the street.
A: I'm starving. B: Shall I make some sandwiches?

rules: Use 'was' for I, he, she, it. Use 'were' for you, we, they.
Use 'there was/were' to talk about the existence of things in the past.
Common time expressions: yesterday, last night, last week, ago, then.

vocabulary.meaning: Con yêu, cục cưng (gọi thân mật)
vocabulary.context: How was your test, sweetie?
It was sunny last week.
Marek and Anna were bored last Saturday.
Fifty years ago, there was a park opposite our house.
Was Mark at the circus yesterday?

rules: The past tense of 'have got' is 'had' (without 'got').
Use 'didn't have' for negatives and 'Did... have' for questions.
Use 'could' to talk about general ability in the past.

vocabulary.context: Did the film have a happy ending?
I didn't have breakfast. I was in a hurry.
When Jamie was a young boy, he had roller-skates.
Albert Einstein couldn't spell.
She couldn't climb trees.

rules: We form the regular past simple by adding -ed to the verb.
Spelling: verb ending in -y after a consonant changes to -ied (study -> studied).
Spelling: double final consonant for short verbs ending in consonant-vowel-consonant (stop -> stopped).
Pronunciation: -ed sounds like /t/ after voiceless sounds, /d/ after voiced, and /ɪd/ after /t/ or /d/.

vocabulary.context: Why did the bird cancel his trip?
Because the feather forecast wasn't good.
George tidied his room an hour ago.
Yesterday, Dad ironed the clothes.
We returned home late.

patterns: Subject + Irregular Past Verb (e.g. went, saw, bought, took)
rules: Irregular verbs do not add -ed in the past simple. Each verb changes differently.
Use 'did/didn't' + base verb form for questions and negatives, not the past form.

vocabulary.meaning: Câu cá bằng ruồi nhân tạo
vocabulary.context: I went fly-fishing with my dad yesterday.
He bought us some popcorn.
The lions jumped through hoops.
I even fed the baby penguins.

vocabulary.meaning: Lời xin lỗi, lý do thoái thác
vocabulary.context: Alex wants to find some excuses to return home.

rules: Use 'will' to talk about predictions without definite evidence, on-the-spot decisions, hopes, and expectations.
Use 'will' with verbs like hope, think, believe, expect, probably, perhaps.

vocabulary.context: She will check you.
She will find out everything about you.
I will probably go to Spain next summer.
Perhaps she will come to the party.
A: Mop the kitchen floor. B: OK, I'll help you then.

patterns: Who/Whose/What/Where/When/Why/How/How much/How many + auxiliary + subject + verb?
rules: Use question words to ask for specific information (person, place, time, reason, quantity).

vocabulary.context: How many bees do you need in a bee choir?
A bee choir answers: Sting!
How much does it cost? It's 20 euros.
What nationality are you? Italian.
Whose jacket is this? It's Sara's.

patterns: Irregular: good -> well, fast -> fast, hard -> hard
rules: Adjectives describe nouns and go before nouns or after 'to be'. They remain the same in plural.
Adverbs of manner describe how someone does an action. They usually go after verbs.
Form adverbs by adding -ly to adjectives, noting spelling rules (easy -> easily).

vocabulary.context: Name six wild animals. An elephant is a wild animal.
He is a clever boy. They are clever boys.
All the children behave very well.
Alex is at a summer camp.
Some girls talk very politely to me.

patterns: two syllables ending in -y: -ier than / the -iest
long adjectives: more ... than / the most ...
rules: Use comparative to compare two people, animals, or things.
Use superlative to compare three or more people, animals, or things.
Use 'the' before superlative adjectives.
Irregular: good-better-best, bad-worse-worst, much/many-more-most.

vocabulary.context: Write the comparative and superlative forms.
Superlative adjectives compare three or more things.
In picture A, the lizard is longer than the lizard in picture B.
Write the comparison forms of: slim -> slimmer -> the slimmest.
The hippo in picture A is bigger than the hippo in picture B.

rules: Use 'and' to join two similar ideas.
Use 'but' to join two different/contrasting ideas.
Use 'or' to join two possibilities.
Use 'because' to give a reason.

vocabulary.context: Using conjunctions to join sentences.
You're different because these are special mirrors!
I wanted to go skiing but I didn't have any skis.
I am starving because I didn't have lunch.
Write the six best excuses for not doing your homework.
